name: BPC-157
summary: BPC-157 is a synthetic 15-amino-acid peptide studied mainly in animal models for gut healing, tendon and ligament repair, and inflammation modulation; human clinical trial data is essentially absent and its FDA compounding status is unresolved pending a July 2026 PCAC review.
description: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157) is a synthetic peptide derived from a partial sequence found in human gastric juice, widely discussed in the recovery and longevity space for tendon, ligament, and gut-lining repair. Preclinical rodent and in vitro studies point to angiogenesis promotion, nitric oxide pathway modulation, and anti-inflammatory signaling, but almost no controlled human trial data exists. It is not FDA-approved as a finished drug product; products sold online are labeled research use only (RUO) and are not legally intended for human consumption.
safety: No large-scale human safety trials exist and long-term human safety is unknown. Animal toxicology has not identified major red flags at studied doses, but this does not establish human safety. name: CJC-1295
summary: CJC-1295 is a synthetic growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH) analog engineered for a longer half-life than natural GHRH, commonly combined with Ipamorelin in gray-market protocols; its GH/IGF-1 mechanism has early pharmaceutical-development data behind it, but no FDA approval and an uncertain compounding pathway.
description: CJC-1295 is a synthetic analog of growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH) that acts directly on GHRH receptors in the pituitary, upstream of ghrelin-receptor secretagogues like Ipamorelin. Two distinct, non-interchangeable forms are sold: CJC-1295 with DAC (Drug Affinity Complex), which binds serum albumin for a multi-day half-life and sustained non-pulsatile GH/IGF-1 elevation, and CJC-1295 without DAC (also sold as Mod GRF 1-29), a short-acting form (~30 minute half-life) producing a more natural pulsatile GH release.
safety: The DAC form's sustained, non-pulsatile GH elevation is a meaningfully different physiological exposure than the body's natural pulsatile pattern; sustained supraphysiological GH/IGF-1 exposure is associated with known risks (insulin resistance, growth-factor-related concerns) in the broader GH-axis literature, though CJC-1295-specific long-term human data does not exist. No completed long-term human safety trials exist for either form. RUO sourcing carries the same purity and mislabeling risks as other gray-market peptides.
contraindications: Active cancer or cancer history (growth-hormone-axis stimulation is a theoretical tumor-growth concern)
unsupervised RUO product use without physician oversight
May potentiate insulin or other antidiabetic medications (additive blood-glucose effects) and other GH-axis peptides
no controlled long-term human interaction data exists.

name: GHK-Cu
summary: GHK-Cu is a naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide with a genuinely well-supported topical cosmetic evidence base — including human RCTs for skin firmness, fine lines, and wound healing — plus a separate, much less-evidenced injectable/RUO research-peptide use.
description: GHK-Cu (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine, bound to copper) is a naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide found in human plasma, saliva, and urine, whose concentration declines with age. Unlike most peptides in this series, GHK-Cu has a legitimate, well-established topical cosmetic market: multiple human clinical trials of topical formulations report improvements in fine lines, skin firmness/elasticity, and photodamage markers versus vehicle/placebo. It works by delivering copper as a cofactor for enzymes involved in collagen/elastin crosslinking and antioxidant defense, and by promoting angiogenesis and extracellular matrix remodeling in wound-healing models. A separate injectable/RUO systemic form is also marketed in the research-peptide space for broader anti-aging and tissue-repair claims, but that use case has a much thinner human evidence trail than the topical cosmetic literature.

safety: Topical GHK-Cu has a good safety track record in cosmetic use, with occasional mild irritation reported at higher concentrations. Injectable/systemic GHK-Cu safety data in humans is much more limited than the topical literature, and shares the same RUO sourcing and quality-control caveats as other gray-market peptides. Copper is essential but can be harmful in excess; systemic use should be approached with more caution and ideally physician oversight, particularly for anyone with a condition like Wilson's disease affecting copper metabolism.

name: Ipamorelin
summary: Ipamorelin is a selective ghrelin-receptor agonist (growth hormone secretagogue) with more human clinical data than most peptides in this class, showing reliable dose-dependent GH release with minimal cortisol/prolactin cross-reactivity; downstream body-composition and sleep claims are more extrapolated than proven.
description: Ipamorelin is a synthetic pentapeptide classified as a growth-hormone secretagogue (GHS) — a selective ghrelin receptor (GHS-R1a) agonist that stimulates pulsatile pituitary GH release with relatively minimal effect on cortisol, prolactin, or appetite compared to older secretagogues like GHRP-6. It is often paired with CJC-1295 in combination protocols to produce a larger, more sustained GH pulse. Human studies from its 1990s-2000s pharmaceutical development (including postoperative-ileus research) confirm the core GH-release mechanism, though most sleep/recovery/anti-aging marketing claims are extrapolated from GH physiology rather than Ipamorelin-specific trials.
safety: Short-term human studies have not identified major safety signals at studied doses, but long-term (multi-year) safety data does not exist. Because it stimulates endogenous GH release, theoretical concerns around GH's known effects (insulin sensitivity changes, potential growth-factor-driven proliferation effects) apply, supporting physician-supervised use rather than self-directed protocols. RUO-sourced product quality is not independently verified.
contraindications: Active cancer or cancer history (growth-hormone-axis stimulation is a theoretical tumor-growth concern)
unsupervised RUO product use without physician oversight
May potentiate insulin or other antidiabetic medications (additive blood-glucose effects) and other GH-axis peptides
no controlled long-term human interaction data exists.
dosage: No established consumer dosing protocol; see full guide article for regulatory and safety context.
typical_dosage: No established consumer dosing protocol; see full guide article for regulatory and safety context.
legal_status: Not FDA-approved as a finished drug product. Not removed from Category 2 in the same April 2026 action as BPC-157/TB-500/CJC-1295, but named among peptides expected to move toward Category 1 (compoundable) status per the February 2026 HHS/FDA announcement; formal Federal Register rulemaking was still pending as of mid-2026.
regulatory_federal: No prescription form exists on the market; product is RUO or physician-prescribed compounded (where accessible). Prohibited under WADA's S2 category for competitive athletes.
regulatory_changelog: 2026-02: Named among ~14 of 19 originally Category-2-restricted peptides expected to move back toward Category 1 per HHS/FDA announcement. Mid-2026: Formal Federal Register rulemaking confirming final category not yet published.

name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
summary: PT-141 is the research-chemical name for bremelanotide, a melanocortin receptor agonist with an actual FDA-approved product (Vyleesi) for hypoactive sexual desire disorder in premenopausal women; most gray-market marketing (including to men, for erectile dysfunction) extends well beyond that narrow approved indication on much thinner evidence.
description: PT-141 is the research/gray-market name for bremelanotide, a melanocortin receptor (MC3R/MC4R) agonist that acts on central nervous system arousal pathways rather than the peripheral vascular mechanism used by PDE5 inhibitors like sildenafil. Bremelanotide is FDA-approved as Vyleesi for hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) in premenopausal women, based on Phase 3 trials showing modest but statistically significant improvements in desire. Earlier-phase trials explored it for male erectile dysfunction with mixed results; that use case was not the basis for approval and off-label/RUO marketing for it is not backed by the same trial data.

primary_effects: Central Nervous System Arousal Pathway Activation
mechanisms: Central Nervous System Arousal Pathway Activation
raw_mechanisms: Central Nervous System Arousal Pathway Activation

safety: Bremelanotide can transiently raise blood pressure and, in a subset of patients, cause a notable spike — Vyleesi carries specific dosing-frequency limits partly for this reason, a meaningful consideration for anyone with cardiovascular risk factors. Nausea is one of the most commonly reported trial side effects. Repeated use can cause focal skin/mucosal hyperpigmentation, a known melanocortin-receptor class effect. Off-label uses (male sexual enhancement, general libido) have not been evaluated to the same standard as the approved HSDD indication.
contraindications: known cardiovascular disease (including prior stroke or myocardial infarction or unstable angina)
use in postmenopausal women is not well studied
Significantly reduces oral naltrexone absorption by about 60% -- avoid combining with oral naltrexone for alcohol or opioid use disorder
transient blood-pressure rise may add to other blood-pressure-active or cardiovascular medications.

legal_status: Bremelanotide is FDA-approved as Vyleesi, prescription-only, for HSDD in premenopausal women specifically. name: Semaglutide
summary: Semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist, FDA-approved as Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus, backed by a large multi-trial Phase 3 program spanning weight management, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular risk reduction, and kidney outcomes — a categorically stronger evidence tier than the RUO peptides elsewhere in this series.
description: Semaglutide is a GLP-1 (glucagon-like peptide-1) receptor agonist, FDA-approved under the brand names Ozempic (type 2 diabetes) and Wegovy (chronic weight management), with an oral form marketed as Rybelsus. It mimics the gut incretin hormone GLP-1: stimulating glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppressing glucagon, delaying gastric emptying, and acting centrally on hypothalamic/brainstem receptors to reduce hunger signaling. The pivotal STEP trial program showed roughly 15% average body weight loss over 68 weeks at the 2.4mg/week dose, and the SELECT trial demonstrated reduced major adverse cardiovascular events in adults with cardiovascular disease and overweight/obesity.
safety: Common side effects are nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and constipation, most pronounced during dose escalation. Less common but notable: gallbladder disease, rare pancreatitis, and aspiration risk under anesthesia due to delayed gastric emptying. Carries a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumor risk observed in rodent studies and is contraindicated in anyone with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2. Not for use in pregnancy.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2

delayed gastric emptying can alter absorption of oral medications
may increase levothyroxine exposure when co-administered.

legal_status: FDA-approved prescription drug (Ozempic, Wegovy, Rybelsus); not a controlled substance.
regulatory_federal: FDA-approved and commercially available by prescription. summary: TB-500 is a synthetic fragment of thymosin beta-4 marketed for muscle recovery and connective-tissue repair; the underlying Tβ4 biology has real scientific grounding, but the synthetic fragment sold as TB-500 has thin direct human evidence and an unresolved FDA compounding status pending July 2026 PCAC review.
description: TB-500 is a synthetic fragment of thymosin beta-4 (Tβ4), a naturally occurring peptide found in nearly all human and animal cells. It's marketed primarily around muscle recovery, flexibility, and connective-tissue repair, and is often stacked with BPC-157 in gray-market use despite acting through a different mechanism (actin regulation rather than angiogenesis/NO signaling). It is not FDA-approved for human use; product sold online is almost universally labeled research use only.
safety: No controlled human safety data exists for the synthetic TB-500 fragment. Because Tβ4 supports angiogenesis and cell migration broadly, some researchers urge caution in anyone with a personal or family history of cancer, though this has not been demonstrated for TB-500 in humans. RUO-sourced product carries the same purity, sterility, and mislabeling risks common across the gray-market peptide industry.

name: Tirzepatide
summary: Tirzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, FDA-approved as Mounjaro and Zepbound, showing the largest average weight-loss effect of any approved obesity drug to date (up to ~22.5% body weight at 72 weeks in SURMOUNT-1) and superior HbA1c reduction versus semaglutide in a head-to-head trial.
description: Tirzepatide is a dual GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide) and GLP-1 receptor agonist, FDA-approved under the brand names Mounjaro (type 2 diabetes) and Zepbound (chronic weight management) — the first approved drug in its dual-agonist class. The SURMOUNT trial program demonstrated up to ~22.5% average body weight loss at the highest studied dose over 72 weeks, and the SURPASS-2 head-to-head trial showed superior HbA1c reduction versus semaglutide. SURMOUNT-OSA also demonstrated significant improvement in obstructive sleep apnea severity, now reflected in its expanded approved use.
effects: Dual GIP and GLP-1 Receptor Agonism

mechanisms: Dual GIP and GLP-1 Receptor Agonism

unmapped_mechanisms: Dual GIP and GLP-1 Receptor Agonism
safety: Common side effects are nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, and constipation, dose-dependent and most common during titration. Less common but notable: gallbladder disease, rare pancreatitis, injection-site reactions, and aspiration risk under anesthesia. Carries the same boxed warning as semaglutide for thyroid C-cell tumor risk observed in rodent studies, with the same contraindication for personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN 2. Not for use in pregnancy.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2
Do not combine with other GLP-1 or GIP receptor agonists or DPP-4 inhibitors
may cause additive hypoglycemia with insulin or sulfonylureas
delayed gastric emptying can alter absorption of oral medications
acute pancreatitis has been reported in clinical trials.
dosage: No established consumer dosing protocol; see full guide article for regulatory and safety context.
typical_dosage: No established consumer dosing protocol; see full guide article for regulatory and safety context.
legal_status: FDA-approved prescription drug (Mounjaro, Zepbound); not a controlled substance.
regulatory_federal: FDA-approved and commercially available by prescription. As with semaglutide, the FDA has determined tirzepatide is no longer in shortage, curtailing 503B bulk compounding; compounded access now generally requires individualized physician-documented clinical necessity. Not sold as a research peptide.
regulatory_changelog: 2026: FDA shortage determination resolved for tirzepatide, winding down the compounded-supply era; 503B bulk compounding no longer generally available absent individualized clinical justification.
